Constraint (14) ensures that each inference task can be offloaded to only one RSU. Constraint (15) ensures that each RSU has at most one accuracy level of a certain type of model. Constraint (16) ensures that each inference task can only be routed to the RSU which possesses the corresponding DNN model. Constraint (17) ensures that a GPU is used for inference tasks only if the target RSU is equipped with a GPU. Constraint (18) ensures that the accuracy level of the model on the target RSU satisfies the accuracy requirements of the task. Constraint (19) ensures that the storage capacity required for processing inference tasks on each RSU does not exceed its available storage capacity. Constraint (20) ensures that each inference task is completed within the maximal tolerant response delay.

6 Simulation Results | 6.1 Simulation Setup

In the simulations, we simulate the process in which vehicles generate DNN inference tasks and offload them to RSUs for execution, using various DNN models and real computing devices. All algorithms are implemented in Python 3.10. For each simulation scenario, the results are averaged over 10 runs to ensure reliability. 1. DNN models: Vehicles randomly generate inference tasks, which are initially assigned to RSUs in a stochastic manner. The inference tasks include object detection, image classification and semantic segmentation, each of which is supported by three models with low, medium, and high accuracy levels to meet diverse inference demands. Specifically, YOLOv8s, YOLOv8m, YOLOv8l are used for object detection, SqueezeNet1-1, GoogleNet, EfficientNet-V2-S for image classification, and DeepLabV3Plus-EifficientNet-Lite0, DeepLabV3Plus-EifficientNet-Lite4, DeepLabV3Plus-RegNetY-064 for semantic segmentation. where the models are listed in order of increasing accuracy. All inference tasks take a single image of size 224 \times 224 \times 3 pixels as input. Each inference task generated by a vehicle specifies an accuracy requirement, and only RSUs that deploy models meeting this requirement can execute the corresponding task. 1. Computing units: We use four types of computing units to simulate the heterogeneity of computing resources in RSUs: Intel(R) Core i5-9400f and i7-9700 CPUs, as well as NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 and GeForce RTX 4060 GPUs. Specifically, each RSU is randomly equipped with a CPU, and only a subset of RSUs are randomly equipped with a GPU to provide inference service. 2. Other parameters: The number of RSUs varies within [5, 9], and the default value is 5. The number of RSUs equipped with GPUs within [1, 5], and the default value is 3. The number of tasks varies within [30, 50], and the default value is 40.

An inference request can be processed by different RSUs with varying accuracy level models. For example, a request with a medium accuracy requirement can be processed by the corresponding medium or high accuracy models. Thus we define a binary indicator \theta_{mp} to represent whether the task ajk is processed by model \pi_{mp}. Specifically, \theta_{mp} = 1 indicates that the task is processed by the model and vice versa.
The RSU is able to communicate with each other through a wired connection, so that inference tasks can be offloaded from one RSU to others. This means that the RSU to which an inference task is initially submitted may not be the same as the RSU where the task is ultimately offloaded for execution. Thus, transmission delay is introduced only when the task is offloaded to a different RSU. Let Ttrans denote the transmission delay for offloading the inference task ajk from original RSU ojk to RSU r. a model loading delay. Let Tload be the loading delay for using GPU to process the inference task ajk, and it is defined as follows,
Tload = \theta_{mp} \cdot tload, if z0,jk = 0
Tload = \theta_{mp} \cdot jk \cdot jkr \cdot 1empr, otherwise, (10)
where tload represents the loading time of the \pi_{mp}.
Let Tjk denote the response delay of the inference task ajk. The response delay is defined as the time interval from the submission of the task request to the original RSU to the completion of the task. Thus, the response delay is defined as follows,
Tjk = Ttrans + Tdown \cdot \theta_{mp} + Tload + Tproc + Tqueue, (11)
where Tqueue represents the delay to wait for processing at the task queue on RSUs.

results.question: What is the model loading process when a task is executed on a GPU?
results.ground_truth: Because the GPU cannot directly access the CPU main memory, the corresponding model must first be initialized in the CPU memory and then loaded into the GPU's Video RAM (VRAM).

Our proposed GAP algorithm establishes a task-to-device selection strategy, determining which tasks should be scheduled to GPUs for execution, while further ensuring load balancing across the entire system. The detailed procedure of GAP is described in algorithm 2. Specifically, GAP works as follows.
Step 1. Identify the dominant task type and its maximum accuracy requirement for the current RSU equipped with a GPU (line 2).

retrieved.h1_title: Table 1. Performance comparisons among algorithms for different maximum tolerant delay of tasks (Metric: Number of uncompleted tasks)
retrieved.content:
Table 1. Performance comparisons among algorithms for different maximum tolerant delay of tasks (Metric: Number of uncompleted tasks)

| Maximum tolerant delay | BA-GR | BA-MA | LAMA-GAP |
__TABLE_0__
As the maximum tolerable delay decreases, tasks waiting in the queue for earlier tasks to complete may exceed this limit, leading to task failures (i.e., tasks uncompleted). We gradually reduce the maximum tolerable delay for tasks and compare the number of tasks completed by different algorithms under these conditions. From Table 1, the number of completed tasks for LAMA-GAP is lower than that for BA-MA and BA-GR for all cases. For example, for the case where the maximum tolerant delay is 2, the proposed LAMA-GAP reduces the number of unfinished tasks by 84.9% and 63.1% compared to BA-MA and BA-GR, respectively. This demonstrates the superiority of our algorithm in task scheduling, where rational scheduling mechanisms not only increase task completion quantity but also effectively maintain system load balancing. We have formulated an optimization problem for jointly model deployment and task offloading for DNN inference to achieve load balancing in VEC. The objective is to minimize the maximum load among RSUs in VEC, under constraints of maximum tolerant delay for each inference task, requirements of inference accuracy for each inference task, etc. To solve the formulated problem, we have proposed a two-stage matching algorithm, denoted as LAMA-GAP. Specifically, for the first stage, we have designed a load-aware matching algorithm, denoted as LAMA, to deploy DNN models and offload inference tasks among RSUs. For the second stage, we have developed a GPU-accelerated task processing algorithm, denoted as GAP, to offload inference tasks between CPU and GPU to further achieve load balancing in VEC. We have conducted simulations on the platform. with three kinds of DNN inference tasks, three kinds of DNN models with different accuracy, and four kinds of computation units.

1 Introduction

For load balancing, existing works focus on general
computing tasks [10], [11]. However, these works ignore the characteristics of inference tasks. This leads to that these works cannot be well applied for the scenario for DNN inference in VEC. Thus, this paper systematically investigates the algorithm for jointly model deployment and task offloading for DNN inference in VEC with considering the characteristics of DNN inference tasks and VEC, to achieve load balancing. The contributions of this paper are summarized as follows. 1. We formulate the problem of joint model deployment and task offloading for DNN inference in VEC. The objective is to minimize the maximum load among RSUs in VEC, i.e., achieving load balancing, under constraints of maximum tolerant delay for each inference task, accuracy requirement of each inference task, etc. 2. We propose a two-stage optimization algorithm to solve the formulated problem. For the first stage, a load-aware matching strategy is designed by joint deploying DNN models and offloading tasks among RSUs. For the second stage, a strategy is developed by offloading inference tasks between different computation units, i.e., CPU and GPU, to further achieving load balancing in VEC. 3. We conduct the simulations on the platform with four kinds of computation units, three kinds of DNN inference tasks, three kinds of DNN models with different accuracy for each kind of tasks. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm significantly performs better than the state-of-the-art methods, in terms of the maximum load in VEC.

6 Simulation Results | 6.1 Simulation Setup

The number of DNN models which are pre-deployed on RSUs varies within [3, 7], and the default value is 3. The maximum tolerable delay for all tasks is 4s. However, to verify the difference in the number of tasks completed by different algorithms as the maximum tolerable delay decreases, we also conducted experiments with the parameter range set to [0.5, 2.5]s. The storage capacity of each RSU is 300MB, the transmission rate between RSUs is 120Mbs, and the transmission rate between RSUs and the cloud is 960Mbs. 3. Baseline Algorithms: The proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP is evaluated by comparing with the following benchmarks. - Baseline algorithm BA-MA: Inspired by [27, 28], we customize a baseline algorithm based on matching algorithm, i.e., algorithm BA-MA. In BA-MA, tasks prefer RSUs with lower loads, while RSUs prefer tasks they can fulfill. CPU is prioritized for task execution, as GPU is only effective when processing a sufficient number of the same task type. - Baseline algorithm BA-GR: Inspired by [4,29], we customize a baseline algorithm based on greedy strategy, i.e., algorithm BA-GR. BA-GR selects the RSU with the lowest load for each task, deploying or updating models as needed. Similar to BA-MA, CPU is prioritized for task execution.

All inference tasks take a single image of size 224 \times 224 \times 3 pixels as input. Specifically, each RSU is randomly equipped with a CPU, and only a subset of RSUs are randomly equipped with a GPU to provide inference service. 2. We define a binary indicator hmpr to represent whether RSU r has already cached the model. Specifically, hmpr = 1 denotes that RSU r has cached the mth model with accuracy level p and vice versa. Let Tdown denote the communication delay for RSU r to download the mth model with accuracy level p from the cloud.

If an inference task is offloaded to RSU r, it will occupy storage space of RSU for execution. The deployment of DNN models requires a portion of the storage capacity, the occupied storage capacity sdr is calculated as,
sdr = \sum_{m=1}3 \sum_{p=1}X xmprcmp.
Additionally, let sir denote the input data for the inference tasks also take up storage capacity, which can be calculated as,
sir = \sum_{j=1}N \sum_{k=1}nj yjkrdjk.

Let R = {r | r = 1, 2, \cdot \cdot \cdot , R} denote the set of RSUs. The RSUs are heterogeneous in their storage capacity, computational resources, cached models, and so on. We assume that each RSU is equipped with a CPU. However, only a subset
of RSUs are equipped with a GPU due to resource constraints and operational cost considerations. For a given RSU r, we define gr \in {0, 1} to indicate whether it is equipped with a GPU, while gr = 1 represents that RSU r is equipped with a GPU. Furthermore, the storage resource of RSU r is denoted as smaxr, we use rcpu and frgpu to represent the available computation resources of its CPU and GPU, respectively. Each RSU has a task queue, where tasks are processed in a first-in-first-out (FIFO) manner.
Let V = {v | v = 1, 2, \cdot \cdot \cdot , V } denote the set of vehicles. The vehicle generates a number of inference tasks (e.g., path planning, pedestrian detection, traffic light detection, etc.). Since the limited computational resources, vehicles cannot process inference tasks locally. Instead, they offload these tasks to RSUs within their communication range for execution.

In urban scenarios, vehicles in busy blocks are likely to generate a large number of inference requests, while suburban areas tend to have fewer inference requests due to fewer vehicles passing by. This leads to an imbalance in the load distribution, with some RSUs becoming overloaded, while others have a significant amount of idle resources. Hence, assigning a balancing workload to each RSU is very important to address the issues mentioned above. The workload of each RSU can be expressed as follows. We define Lr as the workload of RSU r, which is equal to the delay to complete all inference tasks scheduled on this RSU. Thus, Lr can be calculated as,
Lr = \sum_{j}\inmax yjkr \cdot Tjk. (12)

6 Simulation Results | 6.1 Simulation Setup

The number of DNN models which are pre-deployed on RSUs varies within [3, 7], and the default value is 3. The maximum tolerable delay for all tasks is 4s. However, to verify the difference in the number of tasks completed by different algorithms as the maximum tolerable delay decreases, we also conducted experiments with the parameter range set to [0.5, 2.5]s. The storage capacity of each RSU is 300MB, the transmission rate between RSUs is 120Mbs, and the transmission rate between RSUs and the cloud is 960Mbs. 3. Baseline Algorithms: The proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP is evaluated by comparing with the following benchmarks. - Baseline algorithm BA-MA: Inspired by [27, 28], we customize a baseline algorithm based on matching algorithm, i.e., algorithm BA-MA. In BA-MA, tasks prefer RSUs with lower loads, while RSUs prefer tasks they can fulfill. CPU is prioritized for task execution, as GPU is only effective when processing a sufficient number of the same task type. - Baseline algorithm BA-GR: Inspired by [4,29], we customize a baseline algorithm based on greedy strategy, i.e., algorithm BA-GR. BA-GR selects the RSU with the lowest load for each task, deploying or updating models as needed. Similar to BA-MA, CPU is prioritized for task execution.

retrieved.content:
Load Balancing for DNN Inference in VEC

framework that models the matching process between two sets of entities with individual preferences. It is widely used in resource allocation and network optimization. The definition of the matching game is as follows. Let I denote the set of tasks and R represent the set of RSUs. The matching game defined over (I, R) has two preference relations ≻a and ≻r that allow each player to indicate preference over all players in the opposite set. The matching relationship \mu reflects the scheduling of tasks on RSUs for execution, representing the association between tasks and RSUs. Formally, \mu is defined as a matching function I \cup R \rightarrow 2I\cupR, satisfying the following requirements:
- \mu(ajk) \subseteq R and |\mu(ajk)| = 1
- \mu(r) \subseteq I and |\mu(r)| = \sum_{j=1}P \sum_{k=1}n yjkr, s.t. (16)-(20) jk \in \mu(r) \Leftarrow\Rightarrow r \in \mu(ajk)
A blocking pair (ajk, r) exists if both task ajk and RSU r prefer each other over their current matches, and this will make the matching unstable. A matching relation \mu is stable if there are no blocking pairs and all tasks are scheduled to RSUs. In the matching process, each player ranks all other players in the opposite set according to their own preferences, thus creating a preference list. The items ranked higher in the list indicate a closer match to the player’s needs. For inference tasks, each task has a specific deadline and tends to be executed on the RSU that minimizes its response delay, thereby ensuring that the task is completed. The task preference list is defined as:
r ≻ajk r^{\prime} \Leftarrow\Rightarrow Tjk(yjkr = 1) < Tjk(yjkr^{\prime} = 1). For RSUs, they aim to execute tasks that result in the minimal increase in their load, ensuring their computational resources are fully utilized without becoming idle, while preventing the load from becoming too high to affect the execution of subsequent tasks.

6 Simulation Results | 6.1 Simulation Setup

In the simulations, we simulate the process in which vehicles generate DNN inference tasks and offload them to RSUs for execution, using various DNN models and real computing devices. All algorithms are implemented in Python 3.10. For each simulation scenario, the results are averaged over 10 runs to ensure reliability. 1. DNN models: Vehicles randomly generate inference tasks, which are initially assigned to RSUs in a stochastic manner. The inference tasks include object detection, image classification and semantic segmentation, each of which is supported by three models with low, medium, and high accuracy levels to meet diverse inference demands. Specifically, YOLOv8s, YOLOv8m, YOLOv8l are used for object detection, SqueezeNet1-1, GoogleNet, EfficientNet-V2-S for image classification, and DeepLabV3Plus-EifficientNet-Lite0, DeepLabV3Plus-EifficientNet-Lite4, DeepLabV3Plus-RegNetY-064 for semantic segmentation. where the models are listed in order of increasing accuracy. All inference tasks take a single image of size 224 \times 224 \times 3 pixels as input. Each inference task generated by a vehicle specifies an accuracy requirement, and only RSUs that deploy models meeting this requirement can execute the corresponding task. 1. Computing units: We use four types of computing units to simulate the heterogeneity of computing resources in RSUs: Intel(R) Core i5-9400f and i7-9700 CPUs, as well as NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 and GeForce RTX 4060 GPUs. Specifically, each RSU is randomly equipped with a CPU, and only a subset of RSUs are randomly equipped with a GPU to provide inference service. 2. Other parameters: The number of RSUs varies within [5, 9], and the default value is 5. The number of RSUs equipped with GPUs within [1, 5], and the default value is 3. The number of tasks varies within [30, 50], and the default value is 40.

3 System Model | 3.2 Details for DNN Inference

It is important to note that RSUs must have the corresponding DNN models to execute inference tasks. We use M = {m | m = 1, 2, \cdot \cdot \cdot , M} to denote the set of all types of DNN models, with each inference request corresponding to one of these models for execution. We consider each DNN model to have three versions with different levels of inference accuracy (low, medium, and high). Let P = {p | p = 1, 2, 3} indicate three accuracy levels, where the elements of the set correspond to low, medium, and high inference accuracy, respectively. Then, we use \pi_{mp} to represent the mth model with accuracy level p. For a particular inference request, there is a trade-off between response delay and inference accuracy for using different models to serve this request. To achieve lower inference latency may sacrifice the corresponding inference accuracy. Thus, choosing the best inference model is a major challenge. Due to the resource constraints of RSUs, we assume that for each type of model, an RSU can deploy at most one accuracy level model of that model type. The workload required for inference by the model \pi_{mp} is denoted as wcpu for the CPU and wgpu for the GPU, the storage space required to store the model is cmp, where m \in M and p \in P. Without loss of generality, higher inference accuracy models require more storage space and longer inference times. Based on the above premises, we define the integer DNN model caching variable as xmpr \in {0, 1} (r \in R, m \in M, p \in P). Specifically, xmpr can be defined as,
1, if model \pi_{mp} is deployed on RSU r,
Let X = {xmpr | r \in R, m \in M, p \in P}. This records all deployment decisions for DNN models. We use A = {Aj | j \in R} to represent all inference requests received by the RSUs in the system, where Aj denotes the set of all requests received by RSU j. Let Aj = {ajk | j \in R, k \in Nj}, where ajk is an inference request received by RSU j.

The preference list of RSU is defined as:
ajk ≻r amn \Leftarrow\Rightarrow ∆Lr(yjkr = 1) < ∆Lr(ymnr = 1),
where ∆Lr(yjkr = 1) denotes the load increment of task ajk when scheduled on RSU r, similarly for ∆Lr(ymnr = 1). Unlike traditional matching algorithms, LAMA incorporates the system load as a key factor, ensuring that tasks are matched to RSUs in a way that promotes load balancing across the system, thereby deriving model deployment and task offloading strategies. The detailed procedure of LAMA is described in algorithm 1. Specifically, LAMA works as follows. 1. Initialize the set of unmatched tasks and the maximum load of the system, then sort tasks by deadline (line 2). 2. Construct preference lists for all unmatched tasks and RSUs. Each task selects its most preferred RSU (line 4 to line 9).

4 Problem Formulation

Constraint (19) ensures that the storage capacity required for processing inference tasks on each RSU does not exceed its available storage capacity. Constraint (20) ensures that each inference task is completed within the maximal tolerant response delay.

results.ground_truth: Transmission delay is only generated when an inference task is offloaded to an RSU different from the one to which it was initially submitted.

retrieved.h1_title: Table 1. Performance comparisons among algorithms for different maximum tolerant delay of tasks (Metric: Number of uncompleted tasks)
retrieved.content:
Table 1. Performance comparisons among algorithms for different maximum tolerant delay of tasks (Metric: Number of uncompleted tasks)

| Maximum tolerant delay | BA-GR | BA-MA | LAMA-GAP |
__TABLE_0__
As the maximum tolerable delay decreases, tasks waiting in the queue for earlier tasks to complete may exceed this limit, leading to task failures (i.e., tasks uncompleted). We gradually reduce the maximum tolerable delay for tasks and compare the number of tasks completed by different algorithms under these conditions. From Table 1, the number of completed tasks for LAMA-GAP is lower than that for BA-MA and BA-GR for all cases. For example, for the case where the maximum tolerant delay is 2, the proposed LAMA-GAP reduces the number of unfinished tasks by 84.9% and 63.1% compared to BA-MA and BA-GR, respectively. This demonstrates the superiority of our algorithm in task scheduling, where rational scheduling mechanisms not only increase task completion quantity but also effectively maintain system load balancing. We have formulated an optimization problem for jointly model deployment and task offloading for DNN inference to achieve load balancing in VEC. The objective is to minimize the maximum load among RSUs in VEC, under constraints of maximum tolerant delay for each inference task, requirements of inference accuracy for each inference task, etc. To solve the formulated problem, we have proposed a two-stage matching algorithm, denoted as LAMA-GAP. Specifically, for the first stage, we have designed a load-aware matching algorithm, denoted as LAMA, to deploy DNN models and offload inference tasks among RSUs. For the second stage, we have developed a GPU-accelerated task processing algorithm, denoted as GAP, to offload inference tasks between CPU and GPU to further achieve load balancing in VEC. We have conducted simulations on the platform. with three kinds of DNN inference tasks, three kinds of DNN models with different accuracy, and four kinds of computation units.

1 Introduction

Task offloading refers to scheduling an inference task from one RSU to another with more computing resources to accelerate the execution, thereby improving the QoS for inference tasks [7]. Jointly optimizing model deployment and task offloading can more effectively enhance QoS. In the optimization process, these two decisions are coupled. Specifically, the decision on one directly affects the outcome of the other. Model deployment is influenced by distribution of inference tasks, while task offloading depends on whether the required model is available at the target RSU. Therefore, coordinating both is crucial to improve QoS for inference tasks. In VEC, load balancing can effectively optimize the utilization of system computing resources and improve system throughput [8]. The computational resources of RSUs are limited. When a large number of inference tasks arrive simultaneously, the computational resources of RSUs are under significant pressure, resulting in a bottleneck of processing capacity. In this case, the lack of an effective load balancing mechanism may make some RSUs overloaded while others remain underutilized. This imbalance not only increases inference task response delay but also degrades the overall system performance [9]. To address this issue, load balancing strategies for DNN inference in VEC are crucial. However, this is challenging due to several factors. First, RSUs often have different computing units (e.g., CPUs and GPUs) with varying processing powers. Second, inference tasks are unpredictable and delay-sensitive. These factors make load balancing for DNN inference in VEC more difficult than in traditional edge computing environments. In conclusion, joint optimization of model deployment and task offloading can effectively improve the QoS of inference tasks. However, few existing works focus on load balancing for DNN inference in VEC, which results in low resource utilization and poor QoS.

Let R = {r | r = 1, 2, \cdot \cdot \cdot , R} denote the set of RSUs. The RSUs are heterogeneous in their storage capacity, computational resources, cached models, and so on. We assume that each RSU is equipped with a CPU. However, only a subset
of RSUs are equipped with a GPU due to resource constraints and operational cost considerations. For a given RSU r, we define gr \in {0, 1} to indicate whether it is equipped with a GPU, while gr = 1 represents that RSU r is equipped with a GPU. Furthermore, the storage resource of RSU r is denoted as smaxr, we use rcpu and frgpu to represent the available computation resources of its CPU and GPU, respectively. Each RSU has a task queue, where tasks are processed in a first-in-first-out (FIFO) manner.
Let V = {v | v = 1, 2, \cdot \cdot \cdot , V } denote the set of vehicles. The vehicle generates a number of inference tasks (e.g., path planning, pedestrian detection, traffic light detection, etc.). Since the limited computational resources, vehicles cannot process inference tasks locally. Instead, they offload these tasks to RSUs within their communication range for execution.

Load Balancing for DNN Inference in VEC

The preference list of RSU is defined as:
ajk ≻r amn \Leftarrow\Rightarrow ∆Lr(yjkr = 1) < ∆Lr(ymnr = 1),
where ∆Lr(yjkr = 1) denotes the load increment of task ajk when scheduled on RSU r, similarly for ∆Lr(ymnr = 1). Unlike traditional matching algorithms, LAMA incorporates the system load as a key factor, ensuring that tasks are matched to RSUs in a way that promotes load balancing across the system, thereby deriving model deployment and task offloading strategies. The detailed procedure of LAMA is described in algorithm 1. Specifically, LAMA works as follows. 1. Initialize the set of unmatched tasks and the maximum load of the system, then sort tasks by deadline (line 2). 2. Construct preference lists for all unmatched tasks and RSUs. Each task selects its most preferred RSU (line 4 to line 9).

However, the overall trend of the proposed LAMA-GAP gradually decreases, except for the last data point, where no decline is observed. This may be because, when all RSUs are equipped with GPUs, frequent task migration between RSUs leads to performance degradation. Compared to the baseline algorithms, our proposed approach demonstrates a superior ability to efficiently utilize GPU resources. Fig. 5 shows the performance comparisons between the proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP and baseline algorithms BA-MA and BA-GR, in terms of the maximum load of RSUs, for different numbers of pre-deployed DNN models at the RSUs. From Fig. 5, our proposed LAMA-GAP always performs better than algorithms BA-MA and BA-GR. For example, for the case where the number of pre-deployed DNN models is 3, the proposed LAMA-GAP can achieve an improvement of 39.4% and 33.7% compared with baseline BA-MA and BA-GR, respectively. 3 System Model | 3.2 Details for DNN Inference

It is important to note that RSUs must have the corresponding DNN models to execute inference tasks. We use M = {m | m = 1, 2, \cdot \cdot \cdot , M} to denote the set of all types of DNN models, with each inference request corresponding to one of these models for execution. We consider each DNN model to have three versions with different levels of inference accuracy (low, medium, and high). Let P = {p | p = 1, 2, 3} indicate three accuracy levels, where the elements of the set correspond to low, medium, and high inference accuracy, respectively. Then, we use \pi_{mp} to represent the mth model with accuracy level p. For a particular inference request, there is a trade-off between response delay and inference accuracy for using different models to serve this request. To achieve lower inference latency may sacrifice the corresponding inference accuracy. Thus, choosing the best inference model is a major challenge. Due to the resource constraints of RSUs, we assume that for each type of model, an RSU can deploy at most one accuracy level model of that model type. The workload required for inference by the model \pi_{mp} is denoted as wcpu for the CPU and wgpu for the GPU, the storage space required to store the model is cmp, where m \in M and p \in P. Without loss of generality, higher inference accuracy models require more storage space and longer inference times. Based on the above premises, we define the integer DNN model caching variable as xmpr \in {0, 1} (r \in R, m \in M, p \in P). Specifically, xmpr can be defined as,
1, if model \pi_{mp} is deployed on RSU r,
Let X = {xmpr | r \in R, m \in M, p \in P}. This records all deployment decisions for DNN models. We use A = {Aj | j \in R} to represent all inference requests received by the RSUs in the system, where Aj denotes the set of all requests received by RSU j. Let Aj = {ajk | j \in R, k \in Nj}, where ajk is an inference request received by RSU j.

6 Simulation Results | 6.1 Simulation Setup

The number of DNN models which are pre-deployed on RSUs varies within [3, 7], and the default value is 3. The maximum tolerable delay for all tasks is 4s. However, to verify the difference in the number of tasks completed by different algorithms as the maximum tolerable delay decreases, we also conducted experiments with the parameter range set to [0.5, 2.5]s. The storage capacity of each RSU is 300MB, the transmission rate between RSUs is 120Mbs, and the transmission rate between RSUs and the cloud is 960Mbs. 3. Baseline Algorithms: The proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP is evaluated by comparing with the following benchmarks. - Baseline algorithm BA-MA: Inspired by [27, 28], we customize a baseline algorithm based on matching algorithm, i.e., algorithm BA-MA. In BA-MA, tasks prefer RSUs with lower loads, while RSUs prefer tasks they can fulfill. CPU is prioritized for task execution, as GPU is only effective when processing a sufficient number of the same task type. - Baseline algorithm BA-GR: Inspired by [4,29], we customize a baseline algorithm based on greedy strategy, i.e., algorithm BA-GR. BA-GR selects the RSU with the lowest load for each task, deploying or updating models as needed. Similar to BA-MA, CPU is prioritized for task execution.

results.question: Does the system model allow vehicles to process inference tasks locally?
results.ground_truth: No. Due to limited computational resources, vehicles are not permitted to process inference tasks locally.

6 Simulation Results | 6.1 Simulation Setup

In the simulations, we simulate the process in which vehicles generate DNN inference tasks and offload them to RSUs for execution, using various DNN models and real computing devices. All algorithms are implemented in Python 3.10. For each simulation scenario, the results are averaged over 10 runs to ensure reliability. 1. DNN models: Vehicles randomly generate inference tasks, which are initially assigned to RSUs in a stochastic manner. The inference tasks include object detection, image classification and semantic segmentation, each of which is supported by three models with low, medium, and high accuracy levels to meet diverse inference demands. Specifically, YOLOv8s, YOLOv8m, YOLOv8l are used for object detection, SqueezeNet1-1, GoogleNet, EfficientNet-V2-S for image classification, and DeepLabV3Plus-EifficientNet-Lite0, DeepLabV3Plus-EifficientNet-Lite4, DeepLabV3Plus-RegNetY-064 for semantic segmentation. where the models are listed in order of increasing accuracy. All inference tasks take a single image of size 224 \times 224 \times 3 pixels as input. Each inference task generated by a vehicle specifies an accuracy requirement, and only RSUs that deploy models meeting this requirement can execute the corresponding task. 1. Computing units: We use four types of computing units to simulate the heterogeneity of computing resources in RSUs: Intel(R) Core i5-9400f and i7-9700 CPUs, as well as NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 and GeForce RTX 4060 GPUs. Specifically, each RSU is randomly equipped with a CPU, and only a subset of RSUs are randomly equipped with a GPU to provide inference service. 2. Other parameters: The number of RSUs varies within [5, 9], and the default value is 5. The number of RSUs equipped with GPUs within [1, 5], and the default value is 3. The number of tasks varies within [30, 50], and the default value is 40.

1 Introduction

Recently, the rapid development of machine learning technologies has driven the emergence of a wide range of intelligent vehicle applications, such as pedestrian. This work was supported in part by National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 62202108, Guangdong Natural Science Foundation under Grant Nos. 2024A1515010354, 2023A1515111164, 2023A1515011230, China Postdoctoral Science Foundation under Grant No. 2024M760585. detection [1], autonomous driving [2], and collision avoidance. These applications rely on well-trained deep neural network (DNN) models to perform inference on real-time input data. The process of inference will consume a lot of computing resources. Although the computing capabilities of modern vehicles have been greatly improved, they still struggle to support a large number of the inference requests. We use the Jetson Xavier NX, a common on-board computing device, as an example. When using the ResNet-152 model to infer a one-second video, the Jetson Xavier NX takes approximately 4.5 seconds [3]. However, this processing time is far from sufficient to meet the real-time demands of inference tasks. As a solution, vehicular edge computing (VEC) has emerged as a promising paradigm, where inference tasks can be offloaded to nearby roadside units (RSUs) with abundant computing resources [4]. Effective model deployment and task offloading strategies can significantly improve quality of service (QoS) for inference tasks [5]. Model deployment refers to deploying well-trained DNN models on RSUs to serve the corresponding inference tasks. When the inference task arrives at the RSU, if the requested model is not available, the corresponding model needs to be deployed. This will introduce additional model deployment delay. Therefore, model deployment directly impacts the QoS of inference tasks [6].

3 System Model | 3.2 Details for DNN Inference

It is important to note that RSUs must have the corresponding DNN models to execute inference tasks. We use M = {m | m = 1, 2, \cdot \cdot \cdot , M} to denote the set of all types of DNN models, with each inference request corresponding to one of these models for execution. We consider each DNN model to have three versions with different levels of inference accuracy (low, medium, and high). Let P = {p | p = 1, 2, 3} indicate three accuracy levels, where the elements of the set correspond to low, medium, and high inference accuracy, respectively. Then, we use \pi_{mp} to represent the mth model with accuracy level p. For a particular inference request, there is a trade-off between response delay and inference accuracy for using different models to serve this request. To achieve lower inference latency may sacrifice the corresponding inference accuracy. Thus, choosing the best inference model is a major challenge. Due to the resource constraints of RSUs, we assume that for each type of model, an RSU can deploy at most one accuracy level model of that model type. The workload required for inference by the model \pi_{mp} is denoted as wcpu for the CPU and wgpu for the GPU, the storage space required to store the model is cmp, where m \in M and p \in P. Without loss of generality, higher inference accuracy models require more storage space and longer inference times. 1, if model \pi_{mp} is deployed on RSU r,
Let X = {xmpr | r \in R, m \in M, p \in P}. This records all deployment decisions for DNN models. 6 Simulation Results | 6.1 Simulation Setup

In the simulations, we simulate the process in which vehicles generate DNN inference tasks and offload them to RSUs for execution, using various DNN models and real computing devices. All algorithms are implemented in Python 3.10. For each simulation scenario, the results are averaged over 10 runs to ensure reliability. 1. DNN models: Vehicles randomly generate inference tasks, which are initially assigned to RSUs in a stochastic manner. The inference tasks include object detection, image classification and semantic segmentation, each of which is supported by three models with low, medium, and high accuracy levels to meet diverse inference demands. Specifically, YOLOv8s, YOLOv8m, YOLOv8l are used for object detection, SqueezeNet1-1, GoogleNet, EfficientNet-V2-S for image classification, and DeepLabV3Plus-EifficientNet-Lite0, DeepLabV3Plus-EifficientNet-Lite4, DeepLabV3Plus-RegNetY-064 for semantic segmentation. where the models are listed in order of increasing accuracy. All inference tasks take a single image of size 224 \times 224 \times 3 pixels as input. Each inference task generated by a vehicle specifies an accuracy requirement, and only RSUs that deploy models meeting this requirement can execute the corresponding task. 1. Computing units: We use four types of computing units to simulate the heterogeneity of computing resources in RSUs: Intel(R) Core i5-9400f and i7-9700 CPUs, as well as NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 and GeForce RTX 4060 GPUs. Specifically, each RSU is randomly equipped with a CPU, and only a subset of RSUs are randomly equipped with a GPU to provide inference service. 2. Other parameters: The number of RSUs varies within [5, 9], and the default value is 5. The number of RSUs equipped with GPUs within [1, 5], and the default value is 3. The number of tasks varies within [30, 50], and the default value is 40.

In order to provide better quality of service for inference requests and improve the resource utilization of RSU, we aim to minimize the RSU with the maximum load. To address the aforementioned issues, it is necessary to make optimal decisions on task offloading, model deployment, and the selection of devices (GPU or CPU) for executing inference tasks. Formally, the optimization problem P is formulated as:

Constraint (14) ensures that each inference task can be offloaded to only one RSU. Constraint (15) ensures that each RSU has at most one accuracy level of a certain type of model. Constraint (16) ensures that each inference task can only be routed to the RSU which possesses the corresponding DNN model. Constraint (17) ensures that a GPU is used for inference tasks only if the target RSU is equipped with a GPU. Constraint (18) ensures that the accuracy level of the model on the target RSU satisfies the accuracy requirements of the task. Constraint (19) ensures that the storage capacity required for processing inference tasks on each RSU does not exceed its available storage capacity. Constraint (20) ensures that each inference task is completed within the maximal tolerant response delay.

retrieved.h2_title: 2.2 Model Deployment and Task Offloading for DNN Inference
retrieved.content:
2 Related Works | 2.2 Model Deployment and Task Offloading for DNN Inference

Efficient model deployment and task offloading are crucial for enhancing the performance of DNN inference tasks. For general service, some existing studies focus on joint service placement and task offloading [18-20], but these works do not consider the characteristics of DNN inference tasks, so these studies cannot be well applied to DNN reasoning task scenarios. There have been a number of works that have investigated model deployment and task offloading for DNN inference tasks. These studies can be divided into two main categories: one is that inference tasks can only be served by one inference model, the other is that inference tasks can be served by different inference models. For the first category, existing studies focus on model partition to improve the QoS of inference tasks by deploying the partitioned models on different devices. A joint DNN partitioning and task offloading scheme is proposed to minimize latency and energy consumption by optimizing partition points and task allocation for multiple devices and edge servers [21]. A framework named MT-A3C is proposed to optimize fine-grained DNN partitioning in mobile edge computing, minimizing inference latency under delay constraints [22]. A method leveraging enhanced particle swarm optimization and adaptive partitioning is proposed to jointly optimize device placement and model partitioning for throughput maximization in heterogeneous edge computing [23]. For the second category, existing studies focus on model selection, model deployment and task offloading. Wang et al. propose a DRL-based approach for dynamic DNN model selection and inference offloading in edge-cloud video analytics, and they investigate its impact on system capacity, inference delay, and overall performance through real-world testbed experiments [24].

results.question: What does constraint (14) in the optimization problem ensure?
results.ground_truth: It ensures that each inference task is offloaded to exactly one RSU for processing.

1 Introduction

Task offloading refers to scheduling an inference task from one RSU to another with more computing resources to accelerate the execution, thereby improving the QoS for inference tasks [7]. Jointly optimizing model deployment and task offloading can more effectively enhance QoS. In the optimization process, these two decisions are coupled. Specifically, the decision on one directly affects the outcome of the other. Model deployment is influenced by distribution of inference tasks, while task offloading depends on whether the required model is available at the target RSU. Therefore, coordinating both is crucial to improve QoS for inference tasks. In VEC, load balancing can effectively optimize the utilization of system computing resources and improve system throughput [8]. The computational resources of RSUs are limited. When a large number of inference tasks arrive simultaneously, the computational resources of RSUs are under significant pressure, resulting in a bottleneck of processing capacity. In this case, the lack of an effective load balancing mechanism may make some RSUs overloaded while others remain underutilized. This imbalance not only increases inference task response delay but also degrades the overall system performance [9]. To address this issue, load balancing strategies for DNN inference in VEC are crucial. However, this is challenging due to several factors. First, RSUs often have different computing units (e.g., CPUs and GPUs) with varying processing powers. Second, inference tasks are unpredictable and delay-sensitive. These factors make load balancing for DNN inference in VEC more difficult than in traditional edge computing environments. In conclusion, joint optimization of model deployment and task offloading can effectively improve the QoS of inference tasks. However, few existing works focus on load balancing for DNN inference in VEC, which results in low resource utilization and poor QoS.

3 System Model | 3.2 Details for DNN Inference

It is important to note that RSUs must have the corresponding DNN models to execute inference tasks. We use M = {m | m = 1, 2, \cdot \cdot \cdot , M} to denote the set of all types of DNN models, with each inference request corresponding to one of these models for execution. We consider each DNN model to have three versions with different levels of inference accuracy (low, medium, and high). Let P = {p | p = 1, 2, 3} indicate three accuracy levels, where the elements of the set correspond to low, medium, and high inference accuracy, respectively. Then, we use \pi_{mp} to represent the mth model with accuracy level p. For a particular inference request, there is a trade-off between response delay and inference accuracy for using different models to serve this request. To achieve lower inference latency may sacrifice the corresponding inference accuracy. Thus, choosing the best inference model is a major challenge. Due to the resource constraints of RSUs, we assume that for each type of model, an RSU can deploy at most one accuracy level model of that model type. The workload required for inference by the model \pi_{mp} is denoted as wcpu for the CPU and wgpu for the GPU, the storage space required to store the model is cmp, where m \in M and p \in P. Without loss of generality, higher inference accuracy models require more storage space and longer inference times. Based on the above premises, we define the integer DNN model caching variable as xmpr \in {0, 1} (r \in R, m \in M, p \in P). Specifically, xmpr can be defined as,
1, if model \pi_{mp} is deployed on RSU r,
Let X = {xmpr | r \in R, m \in M, p \in P}. This records all deployment decisions for DNN models. We use A = {Aj | j \in R} to represent all inference requests received by the RSUs in the system, where Aj denotes the set of all requests received by RSU j. Let Aj = {ajk | j \in R, k \in Nj}, where ajk is an inference request received by RSU j.

A blocking pair (ajk, r) exists if both task ajk and RSU r prefer each other over their current matches, and this will make the matching unstable. A matching relation \mu is stable if there are no blocking pairs and all tasks are scheduled to RSUs. In the matching process, each player ranks all other players in the opposite set according to their own preferences, thus creating a preference list. The items ranked higher in the list indicate a closer match to the player’s needs. For inference tasks, each task has a specific deadline and tends to be executed on the RSU that minimizes its response delay, thereby ensuring that the task is completed. results.question: What is the constraint regarding GPU usage?
results.ground_truth: Constraint (17) ensures that an inference task is only assigned to a GPU if the target RSU is equipped with one.

retrieved.content:
6 Simulation Results | 6.1 Simulation Setup

In the simulations, we simulate the process in which vehicles generate DNN inference tasks and offload them to RSUs for execution, using various DNN models and real computing devices. All algorithms are implemented in Python 3.10. For each simulation scenario, the results are averaged over 10 runs to ensure reliability. 1. DNN models: Vehicles randomly generate inference tasks, which are initially assigned to RSUs in a stochastic manner. The inference tasks include object detection, image classification and semantic segmentation, each of which is supported by three models with low, medium, and high accuracy levels to meet diverse inference demands. Specifically, YOLOv8s, YOLOv8m, YOLOv8l are used for object detection, SqueezeNet1-1, GoogleNet, EfficientNet-V2-S for image classification, and DeepLabV3Plus-EifficientNet-Lite0, DeepLabV3Plus-EifficientNet-Lite4, DeepLabV3Plus-RegNetY-064 for semantic segmentation. where the models are listed in order of increasing accuracy. All inference tasks take a single image of size 224 \times 224 \times 3 pixels as input. Each inference task generated by a vehicle specifies an accuracy requirement, and only RSUs that deploy models meeting this requirement can execute the corresponding task. 1. Computing units: We use four types of computing units to simulate the heterogeneity of computing resources in RSUs: Intel(R) Core i5-9400f and i7-9700 CPUs, as well as NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650 and GeForce RTX 4060 GPUs. Specifically, each RSU is randomly equipped with a CPU, and only a subset of RSUs are randomly equipped with a GPU to provide inference service. 2. Other parameters: The number of RSUs varies within [5, 9], and the default value is 5. The number of RSUs equipped with GPUs within [1, 5], and the default value is 3. The number of tasks varies within [30, 50], and the default value is 40.

retrieved.h1_title: Load Balancing for DNN Inference in VEC
retrieved.content:
Our proposed GAP algorithm establishes a task-to-device selection strategy, determining which tasks should be scheduled to GPUs for execution, while further ensuring load balancing across the entire system. The detailed procedure of GAP is described in algorithm 2. Specifically, GAP works as follows.
Step 1. Identify the dominant task type and its maximum accuracy requirement for the current RSU equipped with a GPU (line 2).

4 Problem Formulation

In order to provide better quality of service for inference requests and improve the resource utilization of RSU, we aim to minimize the RSU with the maximum load. To address the aforementioned issues, it is necessary to make optimal decisions on task offloading, model deployment, and the selection of devices (GPU or CPU) for executing inference tasks. Formally, the optimization problem P is formulated as:
10 H. Cao et al. (P) : min   max       L                                               (13)
- X,Y,Z   r\inR            r
- R Xnj          X
s.t. - X              R yjkr = 1                                 (14)
- X3         xmpr \leq 1,   \forallm \in M, r \in R                      (15)
- yjkr \cdot \theta_{mp} \leq xjk          ,    \forallj \in R, k \in N (16)
- zjk \cdot yjkr \leq gr,        \forallj \in R, k \in Nj                    (17)
- yjkr \cdot \theta_{mp} \cdot xjk          p \geq lmin (18)
- sr \leq smaxr     , \forallr \in R                                   (19)
- Tjk \leq \tau_{max},      \forallj \in R, k \in N                           (20)
- xmpr \in {0, 1},         \forallm \in M, p \in P, r \in R               (21)
- yjkr \in {0, 1},    \forallj, r \in R, k \in Nj                       (22)
- zjk \in {0, 1},     \forallj \in R, k \in Nj                          (23)
Constraint (14) ensures that each inference task can be offloaded to only one RSU. Constraint (15) ensures that each RSU has at most one accuracy level of a certain type of model. Constraint (16) ensures that each inference task can only be routed to the RSU which possesses the corresponding DNN model. Constraint (17) ensures that a GPU is used for inference tasks only if the target RSU is equipped with a GPU. Constraint (18) ensures that the accuracy level of the model on the target RSU satisfies the accuracy requirements of the task.

6 Simulation Results | 6.2 Performance Comparison

In this section, we introduce and discuss the performance of the results obtained from simulations conducted. Fig. 2 shows the performance comparisons between the proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP and baseline algorithms BA-MA and BA-GR, in terms of the maximum load of RSUs, for different numbers of tasks. From Fig. 2, the proposed LAMA-GAP outperforms the baselines BA-MA and BA-GR in terms of the maximum load of RSUs for all cases. For example, for the case where the number of tasks is 50, the maximum load of RSUs for our LAMA-GAP is 39.3% and 35.7% lower than BA-MA and BA-GR, respectively. Fig. 3 shows the performance comparisons between the proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP and baseline algorithm BA-MA and BA-GR, in terms of the maximum load of RSUs, for different numbers of RSUs. As shown in Fig. 3, the

The proposed LAMA-GAP outperforms the two baseline algorithms for all cases. For example, for the number of RSUs is 9, the maximum load of RSUs for LAMA-GAP is 1.6226, for BA-MA is 2.4225, for BA-GR is 2.3111. In other words, the maximum load of RSUs for LAMA-GAP is lower by 33.1% and 29.8%, compared with that for BA-MA and BA-GR. Besides, the performance of all algorithms improves as the number of RSUs increases. This is because a higher number of RSUs provides more available resources, allowing tasks to have more RSU options, which in turn reduces the maximum load of RSUs. Fig. 4 shows the performance comparisons between the proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP and baseline algorithm BA-MA and BA-GR, in terms of the maximum load of RSUs, for different numbers of RSUs with GPUs. As shown in Fig. 4, LAMA-GAP outperforms BA-MA and BA-GR for all cases in terms of the maximum load of RSUs.

results.question: What does the acronym LAMA stand for?
results.ground_truth: Load-Aware Matching Algorithm.

retrieved.h1_title: Load Balancing for DNN Inference in VEC
retrieved.content:
Load Balancing for DNN Inference in VEC

The preference list of RSU is defined as:
ajk ≻r amn \Leftarrow\Rightarrow ∆Lr(yjkr = 1) < ∆Lr(ymnr = 1),
where ∆Lr(yjkr = 1) denotes the load increment of task ajk when scheduled on RSU r, similarly for ∆Lr(ymnr = 1). Unlike traditional matching algorithms, LAMA incorporates the system load as a key factor, ensuring that tasks are matched to RSUs in a way that promotes load balancing across the system, thereby deriving model deployment and task offloading strategies. The detailed procedure of LAMA is described in algorithm 1. Specifically, LAMA works as follows. 1. Initialize the set of unmatched tasks and the maximum load of the system, then sort tasks by deadline (line 2). 2. Construct preference lists for all unmatched tasks and RSUs. Each task selects its most preferred RSU (line 4 to line 9).

retrieved.h1_title: Table 1. Performance comparisons among algorithms for different maximum tolerant delay of tasks (Metric: Number of uncompleted tasks)
retrieved.content:
Table 1. Performance comparisons among algorithms for different maximum tolerant delay of tasks (Metric: Number of uncompleted tasks)

| Maximum tolerant delay | BA-GR | BA-MA | LAMA-GAP |
__TABLE_0__
As the maximum tolerable delay decreases, tasks waiting in the queue for earlier tasks to complete may exceed this limit, leading to task failures (i.e., tasks uncompleted). We gradually reduce the maximum tolerable delay for tasks and compare the number of tasks completed by different algorithms under these conditions. From Table 1, the number of completed tasks for LAMA-GAP is lower than that for BA-MA and BA-GR for all cases. For example, for the case where the maximum tolerant delay is 2, the proposed LAMA-GAP reduces the number of unfinished tasks by 84.9% and 63.1% compared to BA-MA and BA-GR, respectively. This demonstrates the superiority of our algorithm in task scheduling, where rational scheduling mechanisms not only increase task completion quantity but also effectively maintain system load balancing. We have formulated an optimization problem for jointly model deployment and task offloading for DNN inference to achieve load balancing in VEC. The objective is to minimize the maximum load among RSUs in VEC, under constraints of maximum tolerant delay for each inference task, requirements of inference accuracy for each inference task, etc. To solve the formulated problem, we have proposed a two-stage matching algorithm, denoted as LAMA-GAP. Specifically, for the first stage, we have designed a load-aware matching algorithm, denoted as LAMA, to deploy DNN models and offload inference tasks among RSUs. For the second stage, we have developed a GPU-accelerated task processing algorithm, denoted as GAP, to offload inference tasks between CPU and GPU to further achieve load balancing in VEC. We have conducted simulations on the platform. with three kinds of DNN inference tasks, three kinds of DNN models with different accuracy, and four kinds of computation units.

results.question: What is the core idea of a Matching Game?
results.ground_truth: A matching game is a mathematical framework used to model the matching process between two sets of entities, each with its own preferences; it is widely used in resource allocation and network optimization.

retrieved.h1_title: Load Balancing for DNN Inference in VEC
retrieved.content:
Load Balancing for DNN Inference in VEC

framework that models the matching process between two sets of entities with individual preferences. It is widely used in resource allocation and network optimization. The definition of the matching game is as follows. Let I denote the set of tasks and R represent the set of RSUs. The matching game defined over (I, R) has two preference relations ≻a and ≻r that allow each player to indicate preference over all players in the opposite set. The matching relationship \mu reflects the scheduling of tasks on RSUs for execution, representing the association between tasks and RSUs. Formally, \mu is defined as a matching function I \cup R \rightarrow 2I\cupR, satisfying the following requirements:
- \mu(ajk) \subseteq R and |\mu(ajk)| = 1
- \mu(r) \subseteq I and |\mu(r)| = \sum_{j=1}P \sum_{k=1}n yjkr, s.t. (16)-(20) jk \in \mu(r) \Leftarrow\Rightarrow r \in \mu(ajk)
A blocking pair (ajk, r) exists if both task ajk and RSU r prefer each other over their current matches, and this will make the matching unstable. A matching relation \mu is stable if there are no blocking pairs and all tasks are scheduled to RSUs. In the matching process, each player ranks all other players in the opposite set according to their own preferences, thus creating a preference list. The items ranked higher in the list indicate a closer match to the player’s needs. For inference tasks, each task has a specific deadline and tends to be executed on the RSU that minimizes its response delay, thereby ensuring that the task is completed. The task preference list is defined as:
r ≻ajk r^{\prime} \Leftarrow\Rightarrow Tjk(yjkr = 1) < Tjk(yjkr^{\prime} = 1). For RSUs, they aim to execute tasks that result in the minimal increase in their load, ensuring their computational resources are fully utilized without becoming idle, while preventing the load from becoming too high to affect the execution of subsequent tasks.

The preference list of RSU is defined as:
ajk ≻r amn \Leftarrow\Rightarrow ∆Lr(yjkr = 1) < ∆Lr(ymnr = 1),
where ∆Lr(yjkr = 1) denotes the load increment of task ajk when scheduled on RSU r, similarly for ∆Lr(ymnr = 1). Unlike traditional matching algorithms, LAMA incorporates the system load as a key factor, ensuring that tasks are matched to RSUs in a way that promotes load balancing across the system, thereby deriving model deployment and task offloading strategies. The detailed procedure of LAMA is described in algorithm 1. Specifically, LAMA works as follows. 1. Initialize the set of unmatched tasks and the maximum load of the system, then sort tasks by deadline (line 2). 2. Construct preference lists for all unmatched tasks and RSUs. Each task selects its most preferred RSU (line 4 to line 9).

results.question: What does an inference task prioritize when establishing its preference list in LAMA?
results.ground_truth: Tasks prefer RSUs that can minimize their response latency to ensure the task is completed before the deadline.

framework that models the matching process between two sets of entities with individual preferences. It is widely used in resource allocation and network optimization. The definition of the matching game is as follows. Let I denote the set of tasks and R represent the set of RSUs. The matching game defined over (I, R) has two preference relations ≻a and ≻r that allow each player to indicate preference over all players in the opposite set. The matching relationship \mu reflects the scheduling of tasks on RSUs for execution, representing the association between tasks and RSUs. Formally, \mu is defined as a matching function I \cup R \rightarrow 2I\cupR, satisfying the following requirements:
- \mu(ajk) \subseteq R and |\mu(ajk)| = 1
- \mu(r) \subseteq I and |\mu(r)| = \sum_{j=1}P \sum_{k=1}n yjkr, s.t. (16)-(20) jk \in \mu(r) \Leftarrow\Rightarrow r \in \mu(ajk)
A blocking pair (ajk, r) exists if both task ajk and RSU r prefer each other over their current matches, and this will make the matching unstable. A matching relation \mu is stable if there are no blocking pairs and all tasks are scheduled to RSUs. In the matching process, each player ranks all other players in the opposite set according to their own preferences, thus creating a preference list. The items ranked higher in the list indicate a closer match to the player’s needs. For inference tasks, each task has a specific deadline and tends to be executed on the RSU that minimizes its response delay, thereby ensuring that the task is completed. The task preference list is defined as:
r ≻ajk r^{\prime} \Leftarrow\Rightarrow Tjk(yjkr = 1) < Tjk(yjkr^{\prime} = 1). For RSUs, they aim to execute tasks that result in the minimal increase in their load, ensuring their computational resources are fully utilized without becoming idle, while preventing the load from becoming too high to affect the execution of subsequent tasks.

3 System Model | 3.2 Details for DNN Inference

It is important to note that RSUs must have the corresponding DNN models to execute inference tasks. We use M = {m | m = 1, 2, \cdot \cdot \cdot , M} to denote the set of all types of DNN models, with each inference request corresponding to one of these models for execution. We consider each DNN model to have three versions with different levels of inference accuracy (low, medium, and high). Let P = {p | p = 1, 2, 3} indicate three accuracy levels, where the elements of the set correspond to low, medium, and high inference accuracy, respectively. Then, we use \pi_{mp} to represent the mth model with accuracy level p. For a particular inference request, there is a trade-off between response delay and inference accuracy for using different models to serve this request. To achieve lower inference latency may sacrifice the corresponding inference accuracy. Thus, choosing the best inference model is a major challenge. Due to the resource constraints of RSUs, we assume that for each type of model, an RSU can deploy at most one accuracy level model of that model type. The workload required for inference by the model \pi_{mp} is denoted as wcpu for the CPU and wgpu for the GPU, the storage space required to store the model is cmp, where m \in M and p \in P. Without loss of generality, higher inference accuracy models require more storage space and longer inference times. Based on the above premises, we define the integer DNN model caching variable as xmpr \in {0, 1} (r \in R, m \in M, p \in P). Specifically, xmpr can be defined as,
1, if model \pi_{mp} is deployed on RSU r,
Let X = {xmpr | r \in R, m \in M, p \in P}. This records all deployment decisions for DNN models. We use A = {Aj | j \in R} to represent all inference requests received by the RSUs in the system, where Aj denotes the set of all requests received by RSU j. Let Aj = {ajk | j \in R, k \in Nj}, where ajk is an inference request received by RSU j.

In order to provide better quality of service for inference requests and improve the resource utilization of RSU, we aim to minimize the RSU with the maximum load. To address the aforementioned issues, it is necessary to make optimal decisions on task offloading, model deployment, and the selection of devices (GPU or CPU) for executing inference tasks. Formally, the optimization problem P is formulated as:

Constraint (14) ensures that each inference task can be offloaded to only one RSU. Constraint (15) ensures that each RSU has at most one accuracy level of a certain type of model. Constraint (16) ensures that each inference task can only be routed to the RSU which possesses the corresponding DNN model. Constraint (17) ensures that a GPU is used for inference tasks only if the target RSU is equipped with a GPU. Constraint (18) ensures that the accuracy level of the model on the target RSU satisfies the accuracy requirements of the task. Constraint (19) ensures that the storage capacity required for processing inference tasks on each RSU does not exceed its available storage capacity. Constraint (20) ensures that each inference task is completed within the maximal tolerant response delay.

retrieved.h1_title: Table 1. Performance comparisons among algorithms for different maximum tolerant delay of tasks (Metric: Number of uncompleted tasks)
retrieved.content:
| Maximum tolerant delay | BA-GR | BA-MA | LAMA-GAP |
| ---------------------- | ----- | ----- | -------- |
| 2s                     | 8.4   | 20.6  | 3.1      |
| 2.5s                   | 2.4   | 13.8  | 0.2      |
| 3s                     | 0.1   | 5.3   | 0        |
| 3.5s                   | 0     | 0.5   | 0        |
| 4s                     | 0     | 0     | 0        |
As the maximum tolerable delay decreases, tasks waiting in the queue for earlier tasks to complete may exceed this limit, leading to task failures (i.e., tasks uncompleted). We gradually reduce the maximum tolerable delay for tasks and compare the number of tasks completed by different algorithms under these conditions. From Table 1, the number of completed tasks for LAMA-GAP is lower than that for BA-MA and BA-GR for all cases. For example, for the case where the maximum tolerant delay is 2, the proposed LAMA-GAP reduces the number of unfinished tasks by 84.9% and 63.1% compared to BA-MA and BA-GR, respectively. This demonstrates the superiority of our algorithm in task scheduling, where rational scheduling mechanisms not only increase task completion quantity but also effectively maintain system load balancing.
We have formulated an optimization problem for jointly model deployment and task offloading for DNN inference to achieve load balancing in VEC. The objective is to minimize the maximum load among RSUs in VEC, under constraints of maximum tolerant delay for each inference task, requirements of inference accuracy for each inference task, etc. To solve the formulated problem, we have proposed a two-stage matching algorithm, denoted as LAMA-GAP. Specifically, for the first stage, we have designed a load-aware matching algorithm, denoted as LAMA, to deploy DNN models and offload inference tasks among RSUs. For the second stage, we have developed a GPU-accelerated task processing algorithm, denoted as GAP, to offload inference tasks between CPU and GPU to further achieve load balancing in VEC. We have conducted simulations on the platform.
with three kinds of DNN inference tasks, three kinds of DNN models with different accuracy, and four kinds of computation units. Simulation results have shown that, the proposed LAMA-GAP outperforms two baseline algorithms BA-MA and BA-GR, in terms of the maximum load among RSUs in VEC.

results.question: What does an RSU prioritize when establishing its preference list in LAMA?
results.ground_truth: RSUs prefer to execute tasks that result in the smallest increment to their workload to prevent high loads from affecting subsequent tasks.

6 Simulation Results | 6.1 Simulation Setup

The number of DNN models which are pre-deployed on RSUs varies within [3, 7], and the default value is 3. The maximum tolerable delay for all tasks is 4s. However, to verify the difference in the number of tasks completed by different algorithms as the maximum tolerable delay decreases, we also conducted experiments with the parameter range set to [0.5, 2.5]s. The storage capacity of each RSU is 300MB, the transmission rate between RSUs is 120Mbs, and the transmission rate between RSUs and the cloud is 960Mbs. 3. Baseline Algorithms: The proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP is evaluated by comparing with the following benchmarks. - Baseline algorithm BA-MA: Inspired by [27, 28], we customize a baseline algorithm based on matching algorithm, i.e., algorithm BA-MA. In BA-MA, tasks prefer RSUs with lower loads, while RSUs prefer tasks they can fulfill. CPU is prioritized for task execution, as GPU is only effective when processing a sufficient number of the same task type. - Baseline algorithm BA-GR: Inspired by [4,29], we customize a baseline algorithm based on greedy strategy, i.e., algorithm BA-GR. BA-GR selects the RSU with the lowest load for each task, deploying or updating models as needed. Similar to BA-MA, CPU is prioritized for task execution.

In urban scenarios, vehicles in busy blocks are likely to generate a large number of inference requests, while suburban areas tend to have fewer inference requests due to fewer vehicles passing by. This leads to an imbalance in the load distribution, with some RSUs becoming overloaded, while others have a significant amount of idle resources. Hence, assigning a balancing workload to each RSU is very important to address the issues mentioned above. The workload of each RSU can be expressed as follows. We define Lr as the workload of RSU r, which is equal to the delay to complete all inference tasks scheduled on this RSU. Thus, Lr can be calculated as,
Lr = \sum_{j}\inmax yjkr \cdot Tjk. (12)

6 Simulation Results | 6.2 Performance Comparison

In this section, we introduce and discuss the performance of the results obtained from simulations conducted. Fig. 2 shows the performance comparisons between the proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP and baseline algorithms BA-MA and BA-GR, in terms of the maximum load of RSUs, for different numbers of tasks. From Fig. 2, the proposed LAMA-GAP outperforms the baselines BA-MA and BA-GR in terms of the maximum load of RSUs for all cases. For example, for the case where the number of tasks is 50, the maximum load of RSUs for our LAMA-GAP is 39.3% and 35.7% lower than BA-MA and BA-GR, respectively. Fig. 3 shows the performance comparisons between the proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP and baseline algorithm BA-MA and BA-GR, in terms of the maximum load of RSUs, for different numbers of RSUs. As shown in Fig. 3, the

The proposed LAMA-GAP outperforms the two baseline algorithms for all cases. For example, for the number of RSUs is 9, the maximum load of RSUs for LAMA-GAP is 1.6226, for BA-MA is 2.4225, for BA-GR is 2.3111. In other words, the maximum load of RSUs for LAMA-GAP is lower by 33.1% and 29.8%, compared with that for BA-MA and BA-GR. Besides, the performance of all algorithms improves as the number of RSUs increases. This is because a higher number of RSUs provides more available resources, allowing tasks to have more RSU options, which in turn reduces the maximum load of RSUs. Fig. 4 shows the performance comparisons between the proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP and baseline algorithm BA-MA and BA-GR, in terms of the maximum load of RSUs, for different numbers of RSUs with GPUs. As shown in Fig. 4, LAMA-GAP outperforms BA-MA and BA-GR for all cases in terms of the maximum load of RSUs.

This demonstrates the superiority of our algorithm in task scheduling, where rational scheduling mechanisms not only increase task completion quantity but also effectively maintain system load balancing. We have formulated an optimization problem for jointly model deployment and task offloading for DNN inference to achieve load balancing in VEC. The objective is to minimize the maximum load among RSUs in VEC, under constraints of maximum tolerant delay for each inference task, requirements of inference accuracy for each inference task, etc. To solve the formulated problem, we have proposed a two-stage matching algorithm, denoted as LAMA-GAP. Specifically, for the first stage, we have designed a load-aware matching algorithm, denoted as LAMA, to deploy DNN models and offload inference tasks among RSUs. For the second stage, we have developed a GPU-accelerated task processing algorithm, denoted as GAP, to offload inference tasks between CPU and GPU to further achieve load balancing in VEC. We have conducted simulations on the platform. with three kinds of DNN inference tasks, three kinds of DNN models with different accuracy, and four kinds of computation units.

retrieved.content:
Load Balancing for DNN Inference in VEC

framework that models the matching process between two sets of entities with individual preferences. It is widely used in resource allocation and network optimization. The definition of the matching game is as follows. Let I denote the set of tasks and R represent the set of RSUs. The matching game defined over (I, R) has two preference relations ≻a and ≻r that allow each player to indicate preference over all players in the opposite set. The matching relationship \mu reflects the scheduling of tasks on RSUs for execution, representing the association between tasks and RSUs. Formally, \mu is defined as a matching function I \cup R \rightarrow 2I\cupR, satisfying the following requirements:
- \mu(ajk) \subseteq R and |\mu(ajk)| = 1
- \mu(r) \subseteq I and |\mu(r)| = \sum_{j=1}P \sum_{k=1}n yjkr, s.t. (16)-(20) jk \in \mu(r) \Leftarrow\Rightarrow r \in \mu(ajk)
A blocking pair (ajk, r) exists if both task ajk and RSU r prefer each other over their current matches, and this will make the matching unstable. A matching relation \mu is stable if there are no blocking pairs and all tasks are scheduled to RSUs. In the matching process, each player ranks all other players in the opposite set according to their own preferences, thus creating a preference list. The items ranked higher in the list indicate a closer match to the player’s needs. For inference tasks, each task has a specific deadline and tends to be executed on the RSU that minimizes its response delay, thereby ensuring that the task is completed. The task preference list is defined as:
r ≻ajk r^{\prime} \Leftarrow\Rightarrow Tjk(yjkr = 1) < Tjk(yjkr^{\prime} = 1). For RSUs, they aim to execute tasks that result in the minimal increase in their load, ensuring their computational resources are fully utilized without becoming idle, while preventing the load from becoming too high to affect the execution of subsequent tasks.

The preference list of RSU is defined as:
ajk ≻r amn \Leftarrow\Rightarrow ∆Lr(yjkr = 1) < ∆Lr(ymnr = 1),
where ∆Lr(yjkr = 1) denotes the load increment of task ajk when scheduled on RSU r, similarly for ∆Lr(ymnr = 1). Unlike traditional matching algorithms, LAMA incorporates the system load as a key factor, ensuring that tasks are matched to RSUs in a way that promotes load balancing across the system, thereby deriving model deployment and task offloading strategies. The detailed procedure of LAMA is described in algorithm 1. Specifically, LAMA works as follows. 1. Initialize the set of unmatched tasks and the maximum load of the system, then sort tasks by deadline (line 2). 2. Construct preference lists for all unmatched tasks and RSUs. Each task selects its most preferred RSU (line 4 to line 9).

6 Simulation Results | 6.2 Performance Comparison

In this section, we introduce and discuss the performance of the results obtained from simulations conducted. Fig. 2 shows the performance comparisons between the proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP and baseline algorithms BA-MA and BA-GR, in terms of the maximum load of RSUs, for different numbers of tasks. From Fig. 2, the proposed LAMA-GAP outperforms the baselines BA-MA and BA-GR in terms of the maximum load of RSUs for all cases. For example, for the case where the number of tasks is 50, the maximum load of RSUs for our LAMA-GAP is 39.3% and 35.7% lower than BA-MA and BA-GR, respectively. Fig. 3 shows the performance comparisons between the proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP and baseline algorithm BA-MA and BA-GR, in terms of the maximum load of RSUs, for different numbers of RSUs. As shown in Fig. 3, the

The proposed LAMA-GAP outperforms the two baseline algorithms for all cases. For example, for the number of RSUs is 9, the maximum load of RSUs for LAMA-GAP is 1.6226, for BA-MA is 2.4225, for BA-GR is 2.3111. In other words, the maximum load of RSUs for LAMA-GAP is lower by 33.1% and 29.8%, compared with that for BA-MA and BA-GR. Besides, the performance of all algorithms improves as the number of RSUs increases. This is because a higher number of RSUs provides more available resources, allowing tasks to have more RSU options, which in turn reduces the maximum load of RSUs. Fig. 4 shows the performance comparisons between the proposed algorithm LAMA-GAP and baseline algorithm BA-MA and BA-GR, in terms of the maximum load of RSUs, for different numbers of RSUs with GPUs. As shown in Fig. 4, LAMA-GAP outperforms BA-MA and BA-GR for all cases in terms of the maximum load of RSUs.

As the maximum tolerable delay decreases, tasks waiting in the queue for earlier tasks to complete may exceed this limit, leading to task failures (i.e., tasks uncompleted). We gradually reduce the maximum tolerable delay for tasks and compare the number of tasks completed by different algorithms under these conditions. From Table 1, the number of completed tasks for LAMA-GAP is lower than that for BA-MA and BA-GR for all cases. For example, for the case where the maximum tolerant delay is 2, the proposed LAMA-GAP reduces the number of unfinished tasks by 84.9% and 63.1% compared to BA-MA and BA-GR, respectively. This demonstrates the superiority of our algorithm in task scheduling, where rational scheduling mechanisms not only increase task completion quantity but also effectively maintain system load balancing. results.question: How does the algorithm handle an RSU that is in a state of maximum load during LAMA matching?
results.ground_truth: If an RSU has the highest load and not all unmatched tasks prefer it most, the algorithm skips it and continues matching with other RSUs.
